items.q: Why are telescopes often built far from cities?
items.correct: To escape light pollution, which hides faint objects.
items.misconception: Because stars are physically closer in the countryside.
items.random_wrong: Because cities block all radio signals.
items.why: Dark skies let faint objects be seen; the distance to the stars is unchanged by your location.

items.q: What does an object's altitude tell you?
items.why: Altitude is one of two horizontal coordinates fixing direction, not distance or brightness.

items.q: Why look slightly to the side of a faint star (averted vision)?
items.correct: The outer retina is more sensitive to dim light than the centre.
items.why: Rod cells off-centre detect faint light better, so averted vision reveals dimmer objects.

items.q: Altitude in the horizontal coordinate system measures:
items.why: Correct answer: Angle above the horizon. “Compass bearing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many official constellations does the IAU recognise?
items.why: Correct answer: 88. “12” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Light pollution makes observing harder because it:
items.correct: Brightens the sky and hides faint stars
items.why: Correct answer: Brightens the sky and hides faint stars. “Cools the air” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Dark adaptation of the eye typically takes about:
items.why: Correct answer: 20 to 30 minutes. “A few seconds” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly how many minutes does full dark adaptation of the eye take?
items.why: The real figure is 25 minutes. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
